title: Addiction and Depression
author: Caroline

seo.title: Addiction and Depression | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Addiction and Depression. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
What is a depressive disorder?

Depressive disorders can feel like living life underwater, struggling to reach the surface.

Depressive disorders are marked by:

  • Severe sadness, emptiness, or irritability
  • Disinterest in activities that were once pleasurable.

Depressive disorders create significant impairment in an individual’s life. They often lead to isolation and withdrawal.

The DSM recognizes multiple types of depressive disorders. These include:

  • Major depressive disorder Persistent depressive disorder
  • Disruptive mood dysregulation disorder
  • Premenstrual dysphoric disorder
  • Substance/medication-induced depressive disorder
  • Depressive disorder due to another medical condition
  • Recurrent brief depression
  • Short duration depressive episode
  • Depressive episode with insufficient symptoms
  • Unspecified depressive disorder.

How do depressive disorders and addiction overlap?

Alcohol use disorder and depression overlap significantly. According to the National Comorbidity Study (NCS), people with major depression are 1.6 times more likely to develop substance use disorder than people who do not have major depression. People with persistent depressive disorder are 4.1 times more likely to develop an alcohol use disorder and 2.7 times more likely to develop an illicit substance use disorder than people who do not have persistent depressive disorder.

Substance use disorders and depressive disorders overlap significantly. This can be hard both on the individual and on their loved ones.

Treatment of co-occurring addiction and depression

Many depressive disorders respond to pharmacotherapy and/or cognitive-behavioral therapies. In the absence of substance use, we often see improvement in mood after the first month or two. Unfortunately, these therapies are not as effective if the patient is using substances.

Some people attempt to manage depressive disorders with substances for years. Many see it as their only way to consistently experience relief.

It's hard for these individuals to be told that their substance of choice is actually harming them. They’ve formed an attachment to that substance. They have a perception that it relieves them from suffering. It can be difficult to believe that the harm from that substance is greater than the harm from their depressive disorder.

Sobriety begins as an experiment. An individual can start at one month, one week, or one day. They can begin with whatever they are prepared to do. After that trial, the individual may feel more prepared to treat their depression in the absence of substance use.
